news_summary: Astronomers have advanced our understanding of the early universe with the largest 3D map revealing hidden galaxies, while NASA's Van Allen Probe A prepares to re-enter the atmosphere, and new insights suggest space weather may have impacted the search for alien signals.
news_summary_fr: Les astronomes ont fait progresser notre compréhension de l'univers primitif grâce à la plus grande carte 3D révélant des galaxies cachées, tandis que la sonde Van Allen A de la NASA se prépare à rentrer dans l'atmosphère et que de nouvelles découvertes suggèrent que la météo spatiale pourrait avoir eu un impact sur la recherche de signaux extraterrestres.
news_summary_es: Los astrónomos han avanzado en nuestra comprensión del universo primitivo con el mayor mapa tridimensional que revela galaxias ocultas, mientras que la sonda Van Allen A de la NASA se prepara para volver a entrar en la atmósfera y nuevos hallazgos sugieren que el clima espacial podría haber influido en la búsqueda de señales extraterrestres.

articles.title: Astronomers create the largest 3D map of the early universe revealing hidden galaxies
articles.summary: Astronomers have created the largest and most detailed 3D map yet of a glowing signal from the early universe, revealing hidden galaxies and gas from 9-11 billion years ago. By analyzing faint “Lyman-alpha” light emitted by energized hydrogen, scientists used an advanced technique called line intensity mapping to capture not just the brightest galaxies but also the vast cosmic structures surrounding them.
articles.content:
Astronomers working with the Hobby-Eberly Telescope Dark Energy Experiment (HETDEX) have created the most detailed three dimensional map so far of light produced by energized hydrogen in the early universe, dating back roughly 9 billion to 11 billion years. This type of radiation, known as Lyman alpha light, is released in large amounts when hydrogen atoms absorb energy from nearby stars. Because of this property, it serves as a powerful way to locate bright galaxies during this distant period of intense star formation. However, the positions of much dimmer galaxies and clouds of gas that also emit Lyman alpha light have remained mostly hidden.

"Observing the early universe gives us an idea of how galaxies evolved into their current form, and what role intergalactic gas played in this process," said Maja Lujan Niemeyer, a HETDEX scientist and recent graduate from the Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics who led the development of the map. "But because they are far away, many objects in this time are faint and difficult to observe."

To reveal these faint sources, the team used a method known as Line Intensity Mapping. This approach makes it possible to detect the combined glow of many distant objects, helping scientists build a more complete picture of the young universe. The findings were published on March 3 in The Astrophysical Journal.

How Line Intensity Mapping Reveals Hidden Galaxies

Light can be separated into its component wavelengths, producing what scientists call a spectrum. Astronomers study spectra (the plural of "spectrum") by looking for peaks and dips that indicate the presence of particular elements. Instead of identifying individual galaxies one at a time, Line Intensity Mapping measures how strongly a specific element appears across an entire region of space.

"Imagine you're in a plane looking down. The 'traditional' way to do galaxy surveys is like mapping the brightest cities only: you learn where the big population centers are, but you miss everyone that lives in the suburbs and small towns," explained Julian Muñoz, a HETDEX scientist, assistant professor at The University of Texas at Austin, and co-author on the paper. "Intensity mapping is like viewing the same scene through a smudged plane window: you get a blurrier picture, but you capture all the light and not just the brightest spots."

Although Line Intensity Mapping has been used before, this is the first time it has mapped Lyman alpha emission with such a large dataset and such high accuracy. The Hobby-Eberly Telescope at McDonald Observatory gathers enormous amounts of information for HETDEX as it tracks the positions of more than one million bright galaxies in an effort to better understand dark energy. The survey is remarkable not only for its scope but also for its data volume. Researchers have collected more than 600 million spectra from a region of sky spanning an area equivalent to more than 2,000 full Moons.

A Vast Ocean of Untapped Data

"However, we only use a small fraction of all the data we collect, around 5%," explained Karl Gebhardt, HETDEX principal investigator, chair of UT Austin's astronomy department, and co-author on the paper. "There's huge potential in using that remaining data for additional research."

"HETDEX observes everything in a patch of sky, but only a tiny amount of that data is related to the galaxies that are bright enough for the project to use," added Lujan Niemeyer. "But those galaxies are only the tip of the iceberg. There's a whole sea of light in the seemingly empty patches in between."

Supercomputers Reveal the Hidden Structure of the Cosmos

To build the new map, the researchers developed custom software and relied on supercomputers at the Texas Advanced Computing Center. These systems analyzed roughly half a petabyte of HETDEX data. The team then used the known positions of bright galaxies already cataloged by HETDEX to estimate where nearby faint galaxies and glowing gas clouds are located. Because gravity causes matter to cluster together, bright galaxies often mark regions where other objects are likely to be found.

"So, we can use the location of known galaxies as a signpost to identify the distance of the fainter objects," said Eiichiro Komatsu, a HETDEX scientist, scientific director at the Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics, and co-author on the paper. The completed map sharpens the view around bright galaxies while also revealing more detail in the previously unexplored regions between them.

"We have computer simulations of this period," continued Komatsu. "But those are just simulations, not the real universe. Now we have a foundation which can let us know if some of the astrophysics underpinning those simulations is correct."

A New Era of Mapping the Universe

The researchers plan to compare their map with other surveys that examine the same regions of space but focus on different elements. For instance, a Line Intensity Map of carbon monoxide -- which is associated with the dense, cold clouds where stars form -- could help scientists better understand the environments surrounding young stars that produce Lyman alpha radiation.

"This study is a first detection, which is exciting on its own, and it opens the door to a new era of intensity-mapping the universe," said Muñoz. "The Hobby-Eberly is a pioneering telescope. And with new, complementary instruments coming online, we're entering a golden age for mapping the cosmos."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific achievement: the creation of the largest and most detailed 3D map of the early universe using advanced line intensity mapping techniques. This breakthrough reveals previously hidden galaxies and cosmic structures, providing new insights into galaxy formation and the evolution of the universe. The impact is broad, advancing fundamental knowledge in astrophysics with potential to influence future research and understanding of the cosmos.
articles.personality_title: Astronomers create largest 3D map revealing hidden galaxies from early universe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study the early universe to understand how galaxies formed and changed over billions of years. Hydrogen gas in space emits a special type of light called Lyman alpha, which helps locate distant galaxies. However, many faint galaxies and gas clouds have been hard to see until now.

**What happened** – A team of astronomers using the Hobby-Eberly Telescope Dark Energy Experiment (HETDEX) made the biggest and most detailed 3D map of Lyman alpha light from 9 to 11 billion years ago. They used a method called Line Intensity Mapping, which captures the combined glow of many faint objects, not just the brightest ones. This helped reveal hidden galaxies and gas clouds in the early universe.

**Impact** – This map shows parts of the universe that were invisible before, giving a clearer picture of where galaxies and gas clouds lived long ago. It helps scientists understand how galaxies grew and how gas between them influenced their evolution. The work uses huge amounts of data and powerful computers, making it a major step forward in studying the cosmos.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to compare this map with others that study different elements in space, like carbon monoxide, to learn more about star-forming regions. New instruments and surveys will build on this work, leading to even better maps and deeper knowledge of the universe’s history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using advanced techniques, astronomers have created the largest 3D map of the early universe’s faint galaxies and gas, opening new paths to understand how galaxies formed billions of years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronomes créent la plus grande carte 3D révélant des galaxies cachées de l’univers primitif
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient l’univers primitif pour comprendre comment les galaxies se sont formées et ont évolué pendant des milliards d’années. L’hydrogène dans l’espace émet une lumière spéciale appelée Lyman alpha, qui aide à localiser des galaxies très lointaines. Cependant, beaucoup de galaxies faibles et de nuages de gaz étaient difficiles à voir jusqu’à présent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe d’astronomes utilisant l’expérience HETDEX (Hobby-Eberly Telescope Dark Energy Experiment) a créé la plus grande et la plus détaillée carte 3D de la lumière Lyman alpha datant d’il y a 9 à 11 milliards d’années. Ils ont utilisé une méthode appelée cartographie d’intensité de ligne, qui capte la lumière combinée de nombreux objets faibles, pas seulement les plus brillants. Cela a permis de révéler des galaxies cachées et des nuages de gaz dans l’univers primitif.

**Impact** – Cette carte montre des parties de l’univers invisibles auparavant, offrant une image plus claire de l’emplacement des galaxies et des nuages de gaz il y a longtemps. Elle aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les galaxies ont grandi et comment le gaz entre elles a influencé leur évolution. Ce travail utilise une énorme quantité de données et des ordinateurs puissants, ce qui représente un grand progrès dans l’étude du cosmos.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de comparer cette carte avec d’autres qui étudient différents éléments dans l’espace, comme le monoxyde de carbone, pour mieux comprendre les régions où naissent les étoiles. De nouveaux instruments et enquêtes permettront d’améliorer encore ces cartes et d’approfondir la connaissance de l’histoire de l’univers.

**Phrase clé** – Grâce à des techniques avancées, les astronomes ont créé la plus grande carte 3D des galaxies et du gaz faibles de l’univers primitif, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre la formation des galaxies il y a des milliards d’années.
articles.personality_title_es: Astrónomos crean el mapa 3D más grande que revela galaxias ocultas del universo temprano
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian el universo temprano para entender cómo se formaron y cambiaron las galaxias durante miles de millones de años. El hidrógeno en el espacio emite una luz especial llamada Lyman alfa, que ayuda a localizar galaxias muy lejanas. Sin embargo, muchas galaxias débiles y nubes de gas eran difíciles de ver hasta ahora.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de astrónomos usando el Experimento de Energía Oscura del Telescopio Hobby-Eberly (HETDEX) creó el mapa 3D más grande y detallado de la luz Lyman alfa de hace entre 9 y 11 mil millones de años. Usaron un método llamado mapeo de intensidad de línea, que captura el brillo combinado de muchos objetos débiles, no solo los más brillantes. Esto ayudó a revelar galaxias ocultas y nubes de gas en el universo temprano.

**Impacto** – Este mapa muestra partes del universo que antes eran invisibles, dando una imagen más clara de dónde estaban las galaxias y las nubes de gas hace mucho tiempo. Ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo crecieron las galaxias y cómo el gas entre ellas influyó en su evolución. El trabajo usa grandes cantidades de datos y computadoras poderosas, representando un avance importante en el estudio del cosmos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean comparar este mapa con otros que estudian diferentes elementos en el espacio, como el monóxido de carbono, para aprender más sobre las regiones donde se forman las estrellas. Nuevos instrumentos y encuestas mejorarán estos mapas y profundizarán el conocimiento sobre la historia del universo.

**Frase clave** – Usando técnicas avanzadas, los astrónomos han creado el mapa 3D más grande de galaxias y gas débiles del universo temprano, abriendo nuevas formas de entender cómo se formaron las galaxias hace miles de millones de años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast cosmic ocean filled with glowing, interconnected clusters of luminous hydrogen clouds and faint galaxies, softly illuminated by gentle waves of Lyman alpha light, with a symbolic telescope silhouette observing the radiant expanse under a calm, star-speckled sky rendered in natural blues, purples, and soft whites.

articles.title: NASA’s Van Allen Probe A to Re-Enter Atmosphere
articles.summary: NASA’s Van Allen Probe A is expected to re-enter Earth’s atmosphere almost 14 years after launch. From 2012 to 2019, the spacecraft and its twin, Van Allen Probe B, flew through the Van Allen belts, rings of charged particles trapped by Earth’s magnetic field, to understand how particles were gained and lost. The belts shield Earth from cosmic radiation, solar storms, and the constantly streaming […]
articles.content:
NASA’s Van Allen Probe A is expected to re-enter Earth’s atmosphere almost 14 years after launch. From 2012 to 2019, the spacecraft and its twin, Van Allen Probe B, flew through the Van Allen belts, rings of charged particles trapped by Earth’s magnetic field, to understand how particles were gained and lost. The belts shield Earth from cosmic radiation, solar storms, and the constantly streaming solar wind that are harmful to humans and can damage technology, so understanding them is important.

As of March 9, 2026, the U.S. Space Force predicted that the roughly 1,323-pound spacecraft will re-enter the atmosphere at approximately 7:45 p.m. EDT on March 10, 2026, with an uncertainty of +/- 24 hours. NASA expects most of the spacecraft to burn up as it travels through the atmosphere, but some components are expected to survive re-entry. The risk of harm coming to anyone on Earth is low — approximately 1 in 4,200. NASA and Space Force will continue to monitor the re-entry and update predictions.

Originally designed for a two-year mission, the Van Allen Probes A and B launched on Aug. 30, 2012, and gathered unprecedented data on Earth’s two permanent radiation belts — named for scientist James Van Allen — for almost seven years. NASA ended the mission after the two spacecraft ran out of fuel and were no longer able to orient themselves toward the Sun.

The Van Allen Probes were the first spacecraft designed to operate and gather scientific data for many years within the belts, a region around our planet where most spacecraft and astronaut missions minimize time in order to avoid damaging radiation.

The NASA mission, managed and operated by Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Lab, made several major discoveries about how the radiation belts operate during its lifetime, including the first data showing the existence of a transient third radiation belt, which can form during times of intense solar activity.

When the mission ended in 2019, analysis found that the spacecraft would re-enter Earth’s atmosphere in 2034. However, those calculations were made before the current solar cycle, which has proven far more active than expected. In 2024, scientists confirmed the Sun had reached its solar maximum, triggering intense space weather events. These conditions increased atmospheric drag on the spacecraft beyond initial estimates, resulting in an earlier-than-expected re-entry.

Data from NASA’s Van Allen Probes mission still plays an important role in understanding space weather and its effects. By reviewing archived data from the mission, scientists study the radiation belts surrounding Earth, which are key to predicting how solar activity impacts satellites, astronauts, and even systems on Earth such as communications, navigation, and power grids. By observing these dynamic regions, the Van Allen Probes contributed to improving forecasts of space weather events and their potential consequences.

Van Allen Probe B, the twin of the re-entering spacecraft, is not expected to re-enter before 2030.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the successful completion and ongoing scientific impact of NASA's Van Allen Probes mission, which provided critical data on Earth's radiation belts that protect the planet from harmful cosmic radiation and solar storms. This knowledge improves space weather forecasting, benefiting satellite operations, astronaut safety, and terrestrial technologies, representing a meaningful and inspiring advancement in space science with broad societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Van Allen Probe A to Re-Enter Earth’s Atmosphere After 14 Years in Space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA launched two spacecraft, Van Allen Probes A and B, in 2012 to study the Van Allen radiation belts. These belts are rings of charged particles trapped by Earth’s magnetic field that protect us from harmful cosmic radiation and solar storms.

**What happened** – After nearly seven years of collecting important data, Van Allen Probe A is now expected to re-enter Earth’s atmosphere around March 10, 2026. The U.S. Space Force predicts the spacecraft will mostly burn up during re-entry, with a very low chance of any debris causing harm.

**Impact** – The mission lasted much longer than planned and gave scientists valuable information about how the radiation belts change, including discovering a temporary third belt during strong solar activity. This helps improve predictions about space weather, which can affect satellites, astronauts, and even technologies on Earth like GPS and power grids.

**What’s next step** – NASA and the U.S. Space Force will keep tracking the spacecraft’s re-entry and update predictions as needed. Meanwhile, scientists will continue using the data gathered to better understand space weather and protect future space missions and Earth-based systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – After more than a decade in space, NASA’s Van Allen Probe A is returning to Earth, leaving behind crucial data that helps protect satellites, astronauts, and technologies from space weather hazards.
articles.personality_title_fr: La sonde Van Allen A de la NASA va rentrer dans l’atmosphère terrestre après 14 ans dans l’espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a lancé deux sondes, Van Allen A et B, en 2012 pour étudier les ceintures de radiation de Van Allen, des anneaux de particules chargées protégant la Terre des radiations cosmiques et des tempêtes solaires.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Après près de sept ans de collecte de données importantes, la sonde Van Allen A devrait rentrer dans l’atmosphère terrestre vers le 10 mars 2026. L’U.S. Space Force prévoit que la sonde brûlera principalement lors de sa rentrée, avec un très faible risque que des débris causent un dommage.

**Impact** – La mission a duré bien plus longtemps que prévu et a fourni aux scientifiques des informations précieuses sur l’évolution des ceintures de radiation, notamment la découverte d’une troisième ceinture temporaire lors d’une forte activité solaire. Cela aide à mieux prévoir la météo spatiale qui peut affecter satellites, astronautes et technologies terrestres comme le GPS et les réseaux électriques.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA et l’U.S. Space Force continueront de suivre la rentrée atmosphérique de la sonde et mettront à jour leurs prévisions. Les scientifiques poursuivront l’analyse des données pour mieux comprendre la météo spatiale et protéger les futures missions spatiales et systèmes terrestres.

**Phrase clé** – Après plus de dix ans dans l’espace, la sonde Van Allen A de la NASA revient sur Terre en laissant des données essentielles pour protéger satellites, astronautes et technologies des dangers de la météo spatiale.
articles.personality_title_es: La sonda Van Allen A de la NASA reingresará a la atmósfera terrestre tras 14 años en el espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA lanzó dos sondas, Van Allen A y B, en 2012 para estudiar los cinturones de radiación de Van Allen, anillos de partículas cargadas que protegen a la Tierra de la radiación cósmica y las tormentas solares.

**Qué pasó** – Después de casi siete años recopilando datos importantes, se espera que la sonda Van Allen A reingrese a la atmósfera terrestre alrededor del 10 de marzo de 2026. La Fuerza Espacial de EE. UU. predice que la mayoría de la nave se quemará durante la reentrada, con muy bajo riesgo de que los restos causen daño.

**Impacto** – La misión duró mucho más de lo planeado y proporcionó a los científicos información valiosa sobre cómo cambian los cinturones de radiación, incluyendo el descubrimiento de un tercer cinturón temporal durante la alta actividad solar. Esto ayuda a mejorar las predicciones del clima espacial, que puede afectar satélites, astronautas y tecnologías en la Tierra como el GPS y las redes eléctricas.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA y la Fuerza Espacial continuarán monitoreando la reentrada y actualizarán las predicciones según sea necesario. Los científicos seguirán usando los datos para entender mejor el clima espacial y proteger futuras misiones y sistemas terrestres.

**Frase clave** – Tras más de una década en el espacio, la sonda Van Allen A de la NASA regresa a la Tierra, dejando datos clave para proteger satélites, astronautas y tecnologías de los riesgos del clima espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, translucent spacecraft gently descending through vibrant, swirling bands of charged particles representing the Van Allen radiation belts around a softly glowing Earth, with warm golden and soft blue tones conveying protection and discovery, set against a calm cosmic background with subtle hints of solar activity in the distance.

articles.title: SETI may have missed alien signals because of space weather
articles.summary: SETI has spent decades listening for a sharp, well-defined radio signal that could indicate it was sent by distant intelligent life. Now researchers believe that space weather could distort and blur such signals – meaning SETI has been scanning for the wrong thing
articles.content:
SETI has spent decades listening for a sharp, well-defined radio signal that could indicate it was sent by distant intelligent life. Now researchers believe that space weather could distort and blur such signals – meaning SETI has been scanning for the wrong thing

Is there anyone out there? NASA/SDO

We may have been missing signals from intelligent aliens because of solar wind. Researchers from the Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI) Institute say this means we have been watching for the wrong type of signal, potentially failing to spot promising evidence of extraterrestrial life, but the chances of a future discovery are now higher.

The not-for-profit organisation carries out research to help prove the existence of alien life, which includes listening for extraterrestrial radio signals that cannot be explained by natural cosmological phenomena.

Such a signal was previously expected to be a sharp, distinct radio signal in a narrow frequency range. But the new research suggests that such signals that are sent from distant planets may end up being made fainter and wider in the frequency band – essentially blurred slightly – as they pass through the plasma winds of stars.

Vishal Gajjar and Grayce Brown at the SETI Institute calculated the scale of the effect on radio transmissions from spacecraft in our own solar system, then extrapolated that to other stellar environments. They found that a 100 megahertz signal could be widened as much as 100 hertz – enough to “fall below traditional detection thresholds“. A space weather event can similarly increase the amount of broadening experienced by a signal by several orders of magnitude.

Simon George at the SETI Institute says that there is also increasing consensus that looking for narrow radio broadcasts accidentally beamed through space is not the way to spot distant life. “The idea that an intelligent civilisation would send out such signals is becoming dated, especially when you look at how communications and so forth have evolved since the 1960s,” he says. “There has been a dramatic move towards broadband and spread-spectrum techniques as these can carry far more information.”

“One way to view this is to treat Earth like an exoplanet being viewed by an alien civilisation, a theme I often hear around SETI,” says George. “The point is, while Earth was a strong narrowband source in the 1960s, it is much less so now with a continuing downward trend. Of course, if an intelligent civilisation was intentionally sending out a beacon which is designed to be obvious and easy to detect, either for a ‘we are here’ message or some other alien purpose, then that is a different story.”

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

John Elliott at the University of St Andrews, UK, says he chooses to see the news as the glass being half-full, rather than half-empty: it means previous searches may have missed evidence, but also means that future searches will be more likely to succeed.

“It’s over 50 years that we’ve been actively researching and that’s a blink of the eye, isn’t it, when you think about it,” says Elliott. He says that it’s not just the distortion of signals that hampered previous searches, but inadequate technology to spot and extract signals from the noise – something which is changing as computing power and AI becomes more powerful. “Up until recently, we really haven’t had the equipment, the computing power, to do anything really significant. We’ve been grappling around a bit in the dark,” he says. “Project it forward another 1000 years, which is just another heartbeat, can you imagine what our technology is going to be like? It’s going to be magic.”

Eric Atwell at the University of Leeds, UK, was involved with SETI around the turn of the millennium, and quantifies the discovery as perhaps raising a 0.0001 per cent chance of finding an alien signal to 0.0002 per cent.

“It’s still a very low likelihood,” he says. “I don’t think they’ve wasted their time. They have been trying things, and they’ve got pretty strong evidence that what they’re trying doesn’t work, because they haven’t found anything yet.

“What they’re doing is trying to detect strange signals which can’t be put down to known astronomical features, but that’s still a pretty hit-or-miss way of finding intelligent life,” says Atwell.

He is sceptical that passively waiting for telltale evidence of life, accidentally broadcast, is the correct approach if we want to eventually talk to aliens. “If there really are aliens out there, and they want us to find them, they would send us a much more explicit signal,” he says.

Other groups, such as the Messaging Extraterrestrial Intelligence (METI) organisation, have a different approach to finding alien life. They plan to actively broadcast signals to other planets, in case distant life is listening out for signals as we do.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific insight that space weather may have distorted alien signals, explaining why SETI might have missed them. This advances the search for extraterrestrial intelligence by refining detection methods, increasing future chances of discovery. The story has broad significance for humanity's quest to find life beyond Earth and provides detailed scientific context.
articles.personality_title: New research shows space weather may have hidden alien signals from SETI
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence (SETI) has spent over 50 years trying to find radio signals from intelligent life beyond Earth. Scientists expected these signals to be sharp and clear, but so far, no confirmed signals have been found.

**What happened** – Researchers at SETI discovered that space weather, including solar wind and plasma from stars, can blur and weaken radio signals traveling across space. This means the signals SETI has been looking for might be different from what they expected. Instead of sharp signals, alien messages could be stretched out and harder to detect.

**Impact** – This finding changes how scientists should search for alien signals. It explains why earlier efforts may have missed important clues. Also, modern technology and computer power now make it easier to find these blurred signals. This increases the chances that SETI or other groups could detect signs of life in the future.

**What's next step** – SETI and other researchers will adjust their methods to look for wider, weaker signals rather than just sharp ones. They will also use better technology and artificial intelligence to improve their searches. Some groups, like METI, are even sending messages into space, hoping to get a reply.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Understanding how space weather changes alien signals helps scientists improve their search methods and raises the chances of finding intelligent life beyond Earth.
articles.personality_title_fr: La météo spatiale pourrait avoir caché les signaux extraterrestres à SETI
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le programme SETI (Recherche d'intelligence extraterrestre) cherche depuis plus de 50 ans des signaux radio envoyés par une vie intelligente ailleurs dans l'univers. On pensait que ces signaux seraient nets et précis, mais aucun signal confirmé n'a été trouvé jusqu'à présent.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de SETI ont découvert que la météo spatiale, comme le vent solaire et le plasma des étoiles, peut brouiller et affaiblir les signaux radio voyageant dans l'espace. Cela signifie que les signaux que SETI cherchait pourraient être différents : plus larges et plus faibles, donc plus difficiles à détecter.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change la façon dont les scientifiques doivent chercher ces signaux. Elle explique pourquoi les recherches précédentes n’ont rien détecté. De plus, les technologies modernes et l’intelligence artificielle facilitent désormais la détection de ces signaux brouillés, ce qui augmente les chances de succès.

**Prochaine étape** – SETI et d’autres chercheurs vont adapter leurs méthodes pour chercher des signaux plus larges et faibles, et utiliser des outils plus puissants. Certains groupes, comme METI, envoient aussi des messages dans l’espace, espérant une réponse.

**En une phrase** – Comprendre comment la météo spatiale modifie les signaux extraterrestres aide les scientifiques à mieux chercher et augmente les chances de découvrir une vie intelligente au-delà de la Terre.
articles.personality_title_es: La meteorología espacial pudo ocultar señales alienígenas al SETI
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El programa SETI (Búsqueda de Inteligencia Extraterrestre) lleva más de 50 años intentando detectar señales de radio enviadas por vida inteligente en el espacio. Se esperaba que estas señales fueran claras y definidas, pero hasta ahora no se ha encontrado ninguna confirmada.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de SETI descubrieron que la meteorología espacial, como el viento solar y el plasma de las estrellas, puede distorsionar y debilitar las señales de radio que viajan por el espacio. Esto significa que las señales que SETI ha estado buscando podrían ser más anchas y débiles, por lo que son más difíciles de detectar.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia la forma en que los científicos deben buscar estas señales. Explica por qué las búsquedas anteriores no encontraron nada. Además, la tecnología moderna y la inteligencia artificial ahora facilitan detectar estas señales distorsionadas, aumentando las posibilidades de éxito.

**Próximo paso** – SETI y otros investigadores ajustarán sus métodos para buscar señales más amplias y débiles, usando mejores tecnologías. Algunos grupos, como METI, también envían mensajes al espacio con la esperanza de recibir respuesta.

**Una frase clave** – Entender cómo la meteorología espacial cambia las señales alienígenas ayuda a los científicos a mejorar sus búsquedas y aumenta las probabilidades de encontrar vida inteligente fuera de la Tierra.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing a gently glowing radio telescope dish on a calm Earth landscape, with soft, swirling streams of solar wind plasma weaving through space around distant stars, symbolizing the distortion and broadening of alien signals as they travel through stellar space weather.
